1.synthesis:
Judges 15:1–8 narrates the chain of retaliations that grows out of the chapter 14 betrayal: Samson returns to his Philistine wife during wheat harvest with a young goat as a gift. Her father refuses entry: 'I really thought that you utterly hated her, so I gave her to your companion. Is not her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her instead' (v. 2). Samson's response is not negotiation but escalation: 'This time I shall be innocent in regard to the Philistines when I do them harm' (v. 3). He catches three hundred foxes, ties them in pairs by their tails with torches between, and releases them into the standing grain, vineyards, and olive orchards of the Philistines — destroying the entire harvest.

The Philistine response is to burn Samson's wife and her father — the very threat they had used to coerce her cooperation in chapter 14 now executed as punishment for the outcome. Samson answers with a greater slaughter: 'I swore I would be avenged on you, and after that I will quit' (v. 7). He strikes them 'hip and thigh with a great blow' (v. 8) — a Hebrew idiom for total devastating combat — and withdraws to a cleft in the rock of Etam. The entire episode is a personal retaliation spiral: wife's betrayal → Samson's property destruction → Philistine murder of the wife → Samson's mass killing → Philistine military encampment in Judah.
1.historical_context: The wheat harvest timing of Samson's return visit corresponds to April–May in the Palestinian agricultural calendar. Three hundred foxes tied in pairs with flaming torches between their tails — running through standing grain, vineyards, and olive orchards — would have destroyed an entire agricultural season for the region around Timnah. The 'hip and thigh' expression (v. 8) is a hapax legomenon in Hebrew — its precise meaning is debated, but the context makes clear it refers to total, devastating combat. The rock of Etam is unidentified with certainty but was apparently in the Shephelah territory contested between Dan and the Philistines. The burning of the wife and father-in-law by the Philistines fulfills ironically the threat they had used to extract the riddle's answer in chapter 14 — suggesting that Philistine social enforcement was itself brutal and coercive.
1.literary_structure: The unit is constructed as a retaliation chain in five steps: father's refusal → Samson's fox-arson → Philistines burn the wife → Samson's mass killing → Samson's withdrawal. Each step generates the next; no party steps back from the escalation. The Samson cycle's central literary pattern — personal relationships producing national conflict — is most explicit here: a marriage negotiation becomes a campaign that destroys the Philistine harvest and produces a major military confrontation. YHWH's 'opportunity against the Philistines' (Judges 14:4) is advancing through a sequence of personal grudges and betrayals.
1.theological_application: The fox-arson episode makes theologically uncomfortable reading because Samson frames his devastating property destruction as a matter of personal innocence — 'this time I shall be innocent' — based on the Philistines' provocation. The Samson cycle consistently refuses to present its protagonist as simply righteous or simply wicked; he is a person whose legitimate grievances (real betrayals, real provocations) produce responses that themselves cause further harm. The permanent theological principle is that the cycle of personal retaliation — even when initiated by genuine injustice — cannot break itself. It requires an outside intervention. The judge who 'avenges himself' rather than exercising justice within the covenant community's framework deepens the conflict rather than resolving it.
1.christology: The harvest destruction — foxes burning standing grain, vineyards, olive orchards — inverts the covenant's promise of abundance. Deuteronomy 28:51 threatens exactly this loss of grain, wine, and oil as the covenant curse for unfaithfulness. Here YHWH's own judge is the agent of that destruction — but against the Philistines who hold Israel in oppression. The reversal anticipates the pattern in Revelation 14:14–20, where the harvest imagery is explicitly applied to judgment — reaping the earth, treading the winepress of God's wrath. The Samson harvest-destruction is a local, partial enactment of the final harvest-judgment: YHWH using human instruments to strip away the oppressor's prosperity. The grapes of wrath, later and larger, are of the same vintage.

9.pericope_label: The Men of Judah Hand Samson Over
9.synthesis:
Judges 15:9–13 narrates one of the Samson cycle's most revealing episodes: the men of Judah voluntarily bind Samson and hand him to the Philistines. When the Philistines encamp at Lehi in response to Samson's killings, three thousand men of Judah go down to Samson and say: 'Do you not know that the Philistines are rulers over us? What then is this that you have done to us?' (v. 11). Their complaint is not about Philistine oppression but about the disturbance Samson's retaliation has caused. They have so completely accepted Philistine dominance that the deliverer's actions register as a threat, not a liberation.

Samson's response reveals his self-understanding: 'As they did to me, so have I done to them' (v. 11). He is operating entirely within the logic of personal retaliation — a deliverer who does not primarily think of himself as Israel's liberator but as a wronged individual seeking proportional justice. He consents to be bound with two new ropes on the condition that the Judahites themselves will not strike him — and he is delivered bound to the Philistines at Lehi. Three thousand men of Judah, the tribe of David and the tribe of messianic hope, choose compliance with their oppressors and bind their own potential deliverer.
9.historical_context: The Judahites' accommodation to Philistine rule reflects the reality of the late judges period: the Philistine presence in the Shephelah and coastal plain was so established that the tribes of southern Israel had developed a stable, if humiliating, relationship with their overlords. The complaint 'the Philistines are rulers over us' (v. 11) is the same spiritual problem identified in chapter 13 — Israel has stopped recognizing oppression as oppression. Three thousand men going to bind one man, and then delivering him to the enemy, is the judge-period's military and spiritual reversal made literal: the covenant community acting as the oppressor's enforcement arm against its own divinely-appointed deliverer. Lehi means 'jawbone' — the location name foreshadows the weapon that will be used there.
9.literary_structure: The unit functions as an ironic inversion of the rescue narrative: instead of Israel being delivered from the Philistines, Israelites deliver their deliverer to the Philistines. The three-thousand-to-one ratio underscores the absurdity — it takes three thousand men to restrain a single Nazirite who is willing to be restrained. The two ropes that bind Samson in this section are the specific detail that the Spirit's coming in the next section will need to overcome — the narrator is preparing for the Spirit-snap that follows.
9.theological_application: The Judahites' 'do you not know that the Philistines are rulers over us?' is the judge-cycle's clearest statement of the spiritually accommodated community's characteristic response to liberation. The covenant community that has made peace with oppression regards the deliverer not as a rescuer but as a trouble-maker. This is the permanent application: communities that have accommodated injustice often protect that accommodation against those who would disturb it, even when the disturbance is on their behalf. The men of Judah preferred manageable Philistine rule to the risk and disruption of the liberation Samson represented.
9.christology: The three thousand men of Judah binding their deliverer and handing him to his enemies is one of the OT's most pointed anticipations of the passion narrative. John 19:15: 'The chief priests answered, “We have no king but Caesar.”' The religious and civil leadership of Israel, in both cases, chooses accommodation with the occupying power over the deliverer their own covenant tradition had promised. The language of handing over — paradidomi in the NT, the technical term for betrayal-delivery — runs from Judas to the Sanhedrin to Pilate. At Lehi, Judah bound and handed over; at Jerusalem, Judah's leadership handed over again, with the same logic: his actions disturb our arrangement with the rulers over us.

14.synthesis:
Judges 15:14–20 narrates Samson's Spirit-empowered victory at Lehi and its immediate aftermath. As the bound Samson arrives at Lehi, the Philistines shout — and 'the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and the ropes that were on his arms became as flax that has caught fire, and his bonds melted off his hands. And he found a fresh jawbone of a donkey, and put out his hand and took it, and with it he struck 1,000 men' (vv. 14–15). The victory song follows: 'With the jawbone of a donkey, heaps upon heaps, with the jawbone of a donkey I have struck down a thousand men' (v. 16). The word for 'donkey' and 'heap' (hamor and homer) is a wordplay characteristic of Samson's speech — the hero who turns his experiences into riddles and songs.

Immediately after the song, Samson throws away the jawbone and collapses in desperate thirst: 'You have granted this great salvation by the hand of your servant, and shall I now die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?' (v. 18). YHWH splits a hollow place in the jawbone's hill (Lehi, 'jawbone') and water comes out — the spring is named En-hakkore ('spring of the one who called out'). The chapter closes: 'And he judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years' (v. 20). The note comes before the Delilah narrative, not after Samson's death — suggesting the narrator counts Samson's judgeship as spanning the full Philistine period, not ending at his defeat.
14.historical_context: A fresh jawbone of a donkey — supple rather than dried and brittle — was effective as an improvised weapon. The wordplay on hamor (donkey) and homer (heap/pile) in Samson's victory song is untranslatable in English but characteristic of Hebrew punning in the Samson cycle. The En-hakkore spring episode parallels the water-from-the-rock episodes in the wilderness: Exodus 17:6 (Meribah) and Numbers 20:11. The judge who cannot produce water from rock must cry to YHWH, who provides. The twenty-year judgeship note (v. 20) is unusual in its placement — it appears again at the end of chapter 16, bracketing the Delilah narrative as an episode within Samson's judgeship rather than its culmination.
14.literary_structure: The unit has a three-beat structure: Spirit-empowered victory (vv. 14–16), Samson's prayer of thirst and YHWH's provision (vv. 17–19), editorial closure (v. 20). The transition from the victory song to desperate thirst is the cycle's sharpest pivot: in the same scene, Samson is both the mightiest warrior in the narrative and a man who collapses and cries out like a child. The En-hakkore spring makes his thirst the occasion for a theophanic provision — as at Meribah, the cry of need produces a named place of divine response.
14.theological_application: Samson's prayer at En-hakkore — 'You have granted this great salvation... shall I now die of thirst?' — is the Samson cycle's most transparent statement of his covenant relationship with YHWH. He claims the victory as YHWH's gift; he claims his danger as YHWH's problem to solve. There is a directness in his address to YHWH that runs through the Samson narrative despite the Nazirite violations: he knows who gave him his strength, and he prays to that source when the strength is spent. The permanent application is that the recognition of giftedness — 'you have granted this salvation by your servant's hand' — is the proper foundation for continued dependence on the giver rather than self-reliance on the gift. Samson, at his best, knows the distinction.
14.christology: The water from the hollow at Lehi — provided in response to Samson's cry of desperate need after a great victory — echoes forward to John 19:28: 'After this, Jesus, knowing that all was now finished, said (to fulfill the Scripture), “I thirst.”' The greatest victor over the enemies of God's people cries out in thirst after the decisive battle. John 7:37–38 identifies Jesus himself as the rock from which rivers of living water flow — inverting the Lehi pattern: at Lehi, YHWH provides water for the thirsty victor; at the cross, the victor himself becomes the eternal source of the water that satisfies all thirst. The spring named En-hakkore ('spring of the one who called out') points toward the one whose cry of thirst on the cross opened the spring of eternal life.
